replies.author: @fishjones4618
replies.text: On the contrary, TOS was one of the most expensive shows made at that time.  Recency bias has made us think otherwise.  The issue with sci-fi and fantasy is that it everything has to be fabricated from scratch.   Want to do a war movie? Pop over to the local army surplus store.  Legal drama? Go to a Bloomingdale’s  and grab a few Armani suits. Portray a military and scientific expedition in the 23rd century?   Uhhhh Party City? They didn’t just pop over to IKEA pay for a captain’s chair. A production designer was paid to draw it up.  Money was spent on the materials for it, and carpenters fabricated the materials and assembled it. And that’s just one example.  It was still limited. But definitely not cheap.

replies.text:
It’s silly, but others have rightly pointed out that it could be her affectation. A love of history or she’s quirkily old fashioned. Or they are special AR glasses. 

Or, like Kirk, she’s allergic to Retinax V.

We can’t just blindly hate on these shows for things the legacy shows and films did. Like this video body shaming women. Is that legitimately okay again? Has it always been okay? How many male full-on Starfleet officers have we seen who are overweight? Are we to tear down Colm Meaney, Barry Jenner, or William Shatner the same way Critical Drinker dunks on these women? All in the name of “Obesity will be gone in the future, this is unrealistic… for women only.”

There are other things to criticize, let’s please be thoughtful and not give in to the lowest common denominator.

replies.text: @j1p5 Because Colm Meany wasn't actually fat. Did you see him in season one? Wrestling is a very specialized skill set that requires a lot of cooperation from the person you're "fighting," where you can stop and lay down and catch your breath whenever you want. author: @harpiyon
text:
I learned so much from Star Trek, mainly TNG & DS9: 

- Duty & responsibility 
- Professionalism
- Leadership
- Loyalty
- Respect, empathy & trying to understand each other 
- Unity
- Being smart
- Thinking outside of the box
- Dealing with difficult situations 
- Taking hard decisions
- Following your conscience, even if it goes against orders
- Dealing with loss & regret 
- Forgiveness
- Redemption 
And so much more! 

The characters, even the villains, were mostly smart, composed, professional, goal-oriented. 

There were often high stakes, and mistakes or misjudgements had hard, painful consequences. Dialogue & actions were multi-layered and had often deeper meaning than what we saw. Sometimes a character did something rather unusual or risky - just to make a point or to teach someone else a lesson. 

The main cast was smart, disciplined & professional. Not only while on duty, but they were mentally & physically in good shape. Even older characters like Captain Picard. He looked good for his age! They were wearing those tight uniforms that you'd better not wear when you're overweight. 

They were role models: THIS is how you become a useful member of your society. THIS is how you act in difficult situations. THIS is how you behave & interact at work. If you have an important task to do, but a personal conflict with a colleague - you still do the task! 

On the surface, it was Science Fiction with aliens & other planets & robots & space battles... 

In reality it delivered life lessons & wisdom. 

A mature show, written by smart, mature writers for a smart, mature audience. It had brains, but also a heart! 

The actors were phenomenal, to the last side character! They brought the show to life, were believable in their actions & decisions. 

And we loved the characters! We rooted for them, suffered with them, were concerned about them. 

That cliffhanger when the Borg assimilated Captain Picard: We had to wait ONE FULL YEAR to see what would happen next. We were going crazy!  Some thought he'd become the Federation's new arch-nemesis. We were so happy about the resolution. And yet understood that this incident changed him forever. There's a full episode, focusing on him dealing with his trauma. 

I was a teen, so I already understood most things. Rewatching TNG now, decades later, I appreciate it even more. And my wife, who didn't know it before, loves it, too! 

I'm still learning. 

So what exactly can you learn from this new show??? 

I hope Star Trek goes back to producing high quality content. 

We, the older generations of fans, are still here.

Star Trek is like a view inside the human mind, with the main species representing  a different human instinct, quality or weakness. 

Humans = moral superiority complex (as in constantly thinking they're so much better than everyone else) 
Klingon = warrior instinct
Vulcan = cold logic
Borg = Ruthless pursuit of technology 
Ferengi = greed
Romulans = cunning
Cardasians = survivalist

And so on... 

It's like we are watching how a person wrestles with these conflicting thoughts in their mind.

replies.author: @harpiyon
replies.text:
​​@simmer484yes true. And in the end they learn from each other. 
Worf got a little more "human" & "logical", thanks to living among humans & being friends with Data. He was more composed, more tactical than most other Klingons, who often acted on instinct & impulse. 
Spock got a little more human-like. Still cold & logical, but not as much as most other Vulcans. 
Dr. Bashir (DS9) was a bit naive at the start, but thanks to Garak he got a bit more mature. 
Quark (also DS9) appeared like a ruthless greedy bastard - but in fact had a heart of gold. 
At least that's my take on that. The different aspects of a personality - logic, aggression, naivity, greed, etc. - wrestling with each other, until they find balance.

replies.author: @Lukas-Trnka
replies.text:
​@GuardianAngel-u1r I always liked this kind of debates. I think it used to be quite normal among many Star Trek fans before the Kurtzman era.

Anyway, to the topic; I can't remember (and cannot find) that doc Zimmerman would be ever seen with glasses. However, there was for example the Federation president in ST6 with glasses. But he clearly had some special kind of glasses. About the transporter technician, I don't think we should take it that seriously since it was the very first ever attempt to create a Star Trek show and a lot of creative ideas were not yet finalized. This was not a case of clear creative decision _"we want glasses to be part of this universe"._ It was rather a detail that writers didn't thought about in that moment and only later (now intentionally) they chose not to show them anymore. (There were also some protective glasses, but I think those are not really a core of our discussion. And there were glasses weared by less developed aliens, which makes sense.)

Not having glasses in Star Trek is important world building technique. It is one of those small details that (without a need of a line in dialogue) is helping to build the impression for the audience that this sci-fi world is advanced and able to solve problems we cannot solve today. So, from the position of the storytellers, introducing any exceptions really needs to be justified enough. Otherwise, it could be lessening the audience experience.

And about a person's preference; I think that there is a bit of a problem in how people lately started to think about it. I have no problem with respecting someone's preference, however normalizing disabilities as a matter of fashion or expressions of individuality is really dangerous. (Some people do that and it is not ok.) Many of those who already can be offered a safe, affordable and sufficient fix to their disabilities and they reject it - their motivation is actually often not that they are truly happy with their disability. It is more often that they are not mentally and emotionally prepared for such possibility. They are scared of such fundamental change after spending all of their life so far knowing only a life with that disability. And so they rather hide from facing it. Let me tell you, living with a disability (even the smaller one) is nothing to be desired. 

The world of Star Trek is supposed to be better in all ways. That includes not only having technical solutions, but also having an environment where people can get more emotionally balanced so they can face a big change. Also, a person who was born without disability and then acquires it, is likely to want to get it fixed. And those who in Star Trek are born with disability; their parents - in the best interest of their children - are logically doing all they can to fix it as soon as possible so their child can grow up without limitations. Geordi's parents certainly tried that.

text: What's missing here is maturity, as I recall you pointed out in a video a few years back. The writers of original Trek were mature men and women, many of whom had served in the military, raised families, and done other things that had given them the experience, perspective, and scars to write good characters as well as adapt the events of their own lives into engaging episodes of the show. Many had also cut their teeth writing for the pulp magazines at a penny a word, with real competition for that page space. I see very little of that in modern shows.

author: @EvanG529
text:
It needs to be noted that the blue scaly woman is a Jem'Hadar.
The Jem'Hadar are an explicitly all-male species.
Not only that, but Deep Space Nine spent an entire episode drilling into our heads that the Jem'Hadar are genetically engineered to be extremely violent and 100% loyal to the Founders, and that no amount of nurture can overcome their nature.
Not only that, but Jem'Hadar do not eat, they subsist entirely on a drug called Ketracel-White, thus it is also impossible for one to be morbidly obese.

@TheNikoNik   Who are the "good forces"?  And who decides what is a good or evil force?
Psalm 14:1-3
1 The fool says in his heart,
    “There is no God.”
They are corrupt, their deeds are vile;
    *there is no one who does good.*

2 The Lord looks down from heaven
    on all mankind
to see if there are any who understand,
    any who seek God.
3 *All have turned away, all have become corrupt;*
    there is no one who does good,
    not even one.

No man or woman is a "good force" apart from the presence of the Holy Spirit of God indwelling them through faith in Jesus Christ. 
Have you placed your faith in Jesus Christ, renounced your sinful behavior and become a new person through the power of the Holy Spirit living in you?
If not, you are considered evil and corrupt, vile and lacking in understanding.   That is, you are no better than the people you look down on as being evil forces.
